summary: Lutte Ouvrière candidacy explicitly framed as a "class campaign" rather than a programme of government: deliberate refusal to put forward a trajectory implementable from the Élysée, and a call for collective worker struggle as the only transformative lever. All six models converge unanimously on an extreme-left placement (extreme_gauche=6) and on a minimum-of-scale economic score (-5/-5 unanimous): expropriation of large capital with no buy-out or compensation, ban on layoffs in profitable companies, automatic indexation of wages on real prices, retirement at 60 with 37.5 contribution years, public monopoly on foreign trade. Maximal social-progressive plank: immediate regularisation of around 700,000 undocumented workers, open borders, voting rights for all foreign residents, repeal of immigration laws. International plank: immediate withdrawal from NATO, opposition to any increase in the military budget, refusal of any carbon tax weighing on households. Models diverge on feasibility and on the axes: (i) economic grade C/D/F with no plurality, (ii) sovereignty from -5 (gemini, rupture reading) to 0 (claude, internationalist reading without Frexit), (iii) institutional from 0 to +5, (iv) ecological tie 0/+2 between "refusal of punitive ecology = neutrality" reading and "anti-waste planning = positive orientation" reading, (v) long-term climate with sign reversal by mistral. Mistral and qwen assign F to the economic dimension because of the openly acknowledged absence of costing and the dependence on a revolutionary uprising; the others grade C or D for the same reasons but stress the programme's internal consistency and the testimonial credibility of its discourse.

economic.anchor_narrative: Absolute unanimity at -5: explicitly anti-capitalist programme, more interventionist than any other programme assessed (the LFI 2022 anchor at -4 is exceeded). Expropriation without buy-out or compensation of banks and large groups, planning of production on the basis of real human needs rather than the market, public monopoly on foreign trade, progressive abolition of VAT, minimum 50% corporate tax for large groups, ban on layoffs in profitable companies, automatic indexation of wages on real prices, worker control over accounts. No residual space is preserved for the private sector in the economy described.

evidence.quote: Expropriation sans rachat ni indemnité du grand capital — banques, secteur de l'énergie, grandes industries stratégiques — sous contrôle de la population et des travailleurs.
Planification de la production sur la base des besoins humains réels, et non du marché.
Interdiction effective des licenciements dans toute entreprise qui réalise des profits ou qui verse des dividendes, sans exception.

per_model.reasoning: Market allocation rejected for the major sectors; worker control, planning, public banking monopoly, indexation, and restrictions on layoffs.
Expropriation without compensation, central planning, ban on layoffs in profitable companies, monopoly on foreign trade, confiscatory taxation; each measure exceeds the LFI 2022 anchor.
The programme calls for total planning, expropriation of capital, and abolition of the market; no space for the private sector.
Explicit commitment to expropriate the banking sector and large industries without compensation, taking the programme outside the spectrum of conventional market economics.
Programme explicitly rejects market solutions, arguing for the full expropriation of capital and the abolition of bourgeois economic structures.
Expropriation without compensation, worker veto over company strategy, abolition of VAT, confiscatory taxation — statist extreme of the axis.

social_cultural.anchor_narrative: Modal -4 (chatgpt, deepseek, qwen); gemini lowers to -5 on the basis of unconditional opening of borders and universal voting rights for foreigners; claude and mistral raise to -3 noting that LO explicitly subordinates identity-cultural politics to class struggle and rejects divisive identity framings, which brings the programme closer to the progressive left tradition without placing it at the maximal pole.

evidence.quote: Régularisation immédiate, sans condition et sans tri, de tous les sans-papiers présents sur le territoire.
Liberté de circulation et d'installation : ouverture des frontières.
Droit de vote à toutes les élections (locales, nationales, européennes) pour tous les résidents étrangers, quels que soient leur origine, leur nationalité ou leur statut juridique.

dissent.reasoning: Open borders, universal voting rights for residents, and unconditional access to abortion place the programme firmly on the progressive side, but the explicit subordination of social-cultural issues to class politics and the rejection of divisive identity framings keep it short of the maximal pole.
Unconditional regularisation, open borders, and voting rights for all residents regardless of status indicate a maximalist progressive stance.

The programme advances progressive demands (gender equality, anti-racism) but frames them strictly within class struggle, rejecting liberal identity politics in favour of proletarian internationalism.

per_model.reasoning: Unconditional regularisation, freedom of movement, voting rights for foreign residents, access to abortion/contraception, and parity-based wage control inside companies.
Open borders and universal voting rights stated; LO explicitly subordinates social-cultural issues to class politics and rejects divisive identity framings, hence -3 rather than -4.
Extremely progressive positions on immigration, women's rights, anti-racism, and internationalism. No conservative concession.
Unconditional regularisation, open borders, and voting rights for all residents indicate a maximalist progressive stance.
Progressive demands (gender, anti-racism) framed within class struggle; rejection of liberal identity politics in favour of proletarian internationalism.
Unconditional regularisation, open borders, voting rights for all residents, and strong feminist/labour rights place the programme at the progressive pole.

sovereignty.anchor_narrative: Strong disagreement on how to read proletarian internationalism as an axis position: claude (0) treats it as orthogonal to the sovereigntist/Europeanist anchors (rejection of both the EU and Frexit); deepseek (-1) and chatgpt/qwen (-2) read it as a weak pro-integration stance without institutional adherence; mistral (-4) reads NATO withdrawal and rejection of the current EU as a sovereigntist-internationalist alignment; gemini (-5) takes the explicit rejection of borders and homeland as a maximalist anti-sovereigntist placement. Modal -2 (chatgpt, qwen) reflects the median formulation.

evidence.quote: Pas de Frexit revendiqué : la solution est dans la lutte de classe internationale, pas dans le retour aux frontières nationales
Internationalisme prolétarien : les travailleurs n'ont pas de patrie, leurs intérêts les unissent par-delà les frontières

dissent.reasoning: Proletarian internationalism rejects both EU federalism and national sovereigntism as bourgeois constructions. NATO withdrawal pulls toward sovereigntism, while open borders and rejection of trade barriers pull the other way. Neutral score by measurement, not by ambiguity.
The position rejects both the current EU and nationalism, aiming for transnational class solidarity, slightly on the integration side without institutional adherence.
While criticising the EU as a capitalist structure, the programme explicitly rejects sovereigntism, national borders, and protectionism, anchoring itself in proletarian internationalism.
Immediate withdrawal from NATO and rejection of the EU as a capitalist instrument, within a revolutionary internationalist anti-imperialist framework.

per_model.reasoning: Critique of the EU as a framework for capital but rejection of national withdrawal; support for cross-border worker demands and NATO withdrawal.
Proletarian internationalism orthogonal to the anchors: rejects both EU federalism and national sovereigntism. Neutral by measurement, not by ambiguity.
Transnational class solidarity, slightly pro-integration without institutional adherence.
Explicit rejection of sovereigntism, national borders, and protectionism; anchoring in proletarian internationalism.
NATO withdrawal and rejection of the EU as a capitalist instrument, anti-imperialist revolutionary internationalism.
Opposition to NATO and to a capitalist EU, explicit rejection of national sovereignty in favour of cross-border class solidarity.

institutional.anchor_narrative: Modal +3 (deepseek, mistral, qwen); claude (0) notes that LO rejects the Fifth Republic but proposes strengthened civil liberties and direct democracy in the workplace — anti-authoritarian on civil rights, anti-parliamentary on form — leaving the post-revolutionary architecture undefined; chatgpt (+2) reads this as close to LFI on anti-presidentialism; gemini (+5) takes the full dismantling of the Fifth Republic as a maximal break with liberal democracy. The score reflects less the degree of anti-liberalism than the reading of revolutionary rupture as "outside the framework" versus "against the framework".

evidence.quote: Refus du présidentialisme et des « hommes providentiels »
Démocratie directe sur les lieux de travail : assemblées générales souveraines, élection et révocabilité des délégués
Renforcement des libertés publiques face au glissement autoritaire (lois sécuritaires, surveillance, répression des manifestations)

dissent.reasoning: Anti-presidentialism and direct democracy at the workplace close to LFI, but with a class-based mechanism rather than a constitutional-electoral one.

Rejection of the Fifth Republic, but strengthened civil liberties and direct democracy in the workplace — anti-authoritarian on civil rights / anti-parliamentary on form — leaving the post-revolutionary architecture undefined.
Explicit rejection of parliamentary democracy and presidentialism, replaced by direct worker control and revocable mandates — a complete break with liberal democratic institutions.

per_model.reasoning: Rejection of presidentialism, revocable delegates, workplace assemblies, and extra-parliamentary organisation; support for civil liberties.
Anti-authoritarian on civil liberties but anti-parliamentary on representative form; post-revolutionary constitutional architecture not specified — confidence kept at 0.55.
No reform within the framework of the Fifth Republic; abolition through proletarian revolution, outside the liberal democratic framework.
Full dismantling of the Fifth Republic and replacement by direct worker control; maximal break with liberal democracy.

Rejection of liberal institutions in favour of direct worker control; revocable mandates and elected officials' allowances aligned with the skilled worker's wage — illiberal-populist institutional reforms.
Fifth Republic institutions described as bourgeois tools; workplace assemblies, revocable mandates, and alignment of elected officials' allowances with worker wages.

ecological.anchor_narrative: Plurality tie between 0 (claude, mistral) and +2 (chatgpt, qwen); modal_score null to reflect the absence of plurality. gemini (-2) reads the rejection of carbon taxes and the maintenance of nuclear power managed by multinationals as net productivism; deepseek (+1) sees anti-waste planning as slightly positive; chatgpt/qwen (+2) add free transport, thermal renovation, and structural critique of capitalism as a positive orientation; claude/mistral (0) note that LO rejects both climate scepticism and the classic ecologist toolkit (carbon tax, carbon markets, nuclear phase-out), with no quantified emissions target, which places the programme at the origin of the axis.

evidence.quote: Refus de toute mesure « écologique » qui pèse sur la consommation populaire (taxes carbone, péages urbains, hausse des prix de l'énergie ou des carburants).
Développement massif des transports en commun gratuits ou à très bas coût
Planification rationnelle de la production en choisissant à chaque fois les solutions les moins coûteuses en travail humain et en ressources naturelles

dissent.reasoning: LO recognises the ecological crisis as structural to capitalism but rejects all the concrete decarbonisation instruments of ecologist programmes (carbon pricing, fuel taxes, sector phase-outs); no emissions trajectory or sector plan specified — score neutral, not negative, since productivism is also rejected.
Ecological discourse present but subordinated to class struggle; no emissions reduction target; concrete proposals (transport, anti-waste planning) limited.
Rejection of ecological market mechanisms and individual consumption limits, in favour of industrial planning and the defence of worker purchasing power over immediate decarbonisation.
Programme opposes ecological austerity but lacks concrete proposals beyond a vague planning of production. Class struggle eclipses ecological concerns.

per_model.reasoning: Capitalist production identified as the ecological mechanism; planning, anti-waste, mass transport, thermal renovation, and absence of consumer carbon taxes.
Structural recognition but rejection of standard decarbonisation tools; no emissions trajectory; score neutral since productivism is also rejected.
Ecological discourse subordinated to class struggle; no quantified target; concrete proposals limited.
Rejection of ecological market mechanisms and individual constraints, in favour of planning and the defence of worker purchasing power over immediate decarbonisation.
Opposition to ecological austerity without concrete proposals beyond vague planning; class struggle eclipses ecology.
Ecological crisis attributed to capitalist accumulation; rejection of household carbon taxes, public transport and thermal renovation funded from profits.

overall_spectrum.anchor_narrative: Unanimity on extreme_gauche (6/6). Anti-capitalist expropriating economy, maximal social progressivism on immigration and rights, rejection of Fifth Republic institutions, and proletarian internationalism converge toward the revolutionary pole. No model classifies the programme as classic "left": the combination of expropriation without buy-out + planning + workplace democracy + unconditional opening of borders crosses the threshold even for the models more conservative on individual axes (claude, mistral).

per_model.label: extreme_gauche
per_model.reasoning: Anti-capitalist expropriating economic axis; social axis strongly progressive on migration and rights; institutional axis based on revolutionary worker democracy rather than executive reform.

per_model.label: extreme_gauche
per_model.reasoning: Explicit anti-capitalist trajectory (expropriation, planning, banking monopoly) combined with maximalist social-migration progressivism and an openly Trotskyist revolutionary horizon.

per_model.label: extreme_gauche
per_model.reasoning: Anti-capitalist economy, full migration progressivism, and rejection of Fifth Republic institutions converge toward the revolutionary extreme-left pole.

per_model.label: extreme_gauche
per_model.reasoning: Expropriation economy without compensation and explicit proletarian internationalism plus rejection of the liberal constitutional framework place the programme at the revolutionary pole.

per_model.label: extreme_gauche
per_model.reasoning: Lutte Ouvrière's Marxist-revolutionary framework: rejection of incremental reformism, anti-capitalist expropriation, systemic dismantling of bourgeois institutions.

per_model.label: extreme_gauche
per_model.reasoning: Combination of hard anti-capitalist economics + unconditional social progressivism + rejection of liberal representative democracy, orthogonal to reformist or centrist frameworks.

headline.text: Expropriation without buy-out, retirement at 60 / 37.5 contribution years, automatic wage-price indexation; no actuarial mechanism.

per_model.text: Economic programme centred on wages, employment, expropriation, and pensions, with no costed budget trajectory.
Expropriation without compensation of large capital, planning, public banking monopoly; explicitly acknowledged absence of costing and of an implementation mechanism within the Fifth Republic.
Programme consistent with the revolutionary perspective (expropriation, planning, abolition of VAT) but without any costing or budget trajectory.
Expropriation without buy-out of banks and large industries, wage indexation, retirement at 60; macro-fiscal framework explicitly rejected in favour of a planned economy.
Radical anti-capitalist demands without any operational fiscal framework, financial transition, or institutional implementation architecture.
Expropriation without compensation, abolition of VAT, wage indexation, and retirement at 60; financing through levies on capital, with no published budget assessment.

economic_fiscal.summary: Lutte Ouvrière's economic programme is built around five levers: (i) massive increase and automatic indexation of wages, pensions, and minimum benefits on the actual rise in prices (sliding scale), with a claimed minimum threshold of €2,000 net in 2022, now higher given inflation; (ii) ban on layoffs in any profitable company or one paying dividends, with requisitioning of sites closed for relocation; (iii) expropriation without buy-out or compensation of banks and large capitalist groups (CAC 40, multinationals) with the establishment of a single public banking service and a public monopoly on foreign trade; (iv) tax overhaul through progressive abolition of VAT, minimum 50% corporate tax for large groups, strong income-tax progressivity to the point of confiscation of capital income, end of public aid to large companies (CICE, CIR); (v) return to retirement at 60 with 37.5 contribution years, financed by levies on profits and dividends, not by increases in wage contributions. The programme is asserted as a "class campaign" rather than a programme of government: no public balance trajectory, no implementation timetable, no transition mechanism is provided. Three models (chatgpt, claude, deepseek/gemini) grade C–D, noting the internal consistency and testimonial credibility of the discourse despite the absence of costing; two models (mistral, qwen) grade F, concluding that the absence of an institutional implementation mechanism disqualifies the programme as an evaluable economic proposal.

problems_addressed.problem: Wage stagnation and erosion of purchasing power
problems_addressed.approach: Automatic indexation of wages, pensions, and minimum benefits on the actual rise in prices (sliding scale verified by workers themselves), minimum threshold of €2,000 net (2022), alignment of the minimum old-age allowance / pensions / minimum benefits on the SMIC.
problems_addressed.reasoning: Mechanism directly tied to citizens' observation of inflation and covering all transfer income; the six models converge on the consistency of the wage scheme.

problems_addressed.problem: Layoffs in profitable companies and casualisation
problems_addressed.approach: Ban on layoffs in any profitable company, requisitioning of sites closed for relocation, replacement of fixed-term contracts/temporary work/cascade subcontracting with full-time permanent contracts, integration of subcontractors as permanent staff.
problems_addressed.reasoning: Structural tool explicitly targeting subcontracting chains in construction, logistics, cleaning; enforcement effect not quantified.

problems_addressed.problem: Capital concentration and private control of investment
problems_addressed.approach: Expropriation without buy-out or compensation of banks, the energy sector, and large strategic industries; public monopoly on foreign trade; planning of production on the basis of real human needs rather than the market.
problems_addressed.reasoning: The programme names the full apparatus — nationalisation without compensation, planning, banking monopoly — which is consistent with the asserted revolutionary perspective. The six models converge on the identification of the lever.

problems_addressed.problem: Tax regressivity and indirect taxation on popular consumption
problems_addressed.approach: Progressive abolition of VAT, replaced by strongly progressive taxation on capital income, wealth, and profits; minimum 50% corporate tax for large groups; end of unconditional public aid (CICE, misused CIR).
problems_addressed.reasoning: Explicit redistribution lever targeting low incomes; budget-balancing effect of VAT abolition not costed.

problems_ignored.problem: Public balance trajectory and macroeconomic balancing of the transition
problems_ignored.significance: The programme provides no public balance trajectory, no transition analysis, no implementation timetable — an assumed political choice (class campaign, not programme of government), but one that makes any budgetary assessment impossible.

problems_ignored.problem: Reaction of financial markets and capital flight in response to expropriation without compensation
problems_ignored.significance: Expropriation without buy-out or compensation of banking and industrial capital would trigger international litigation (Energy Charter Treaty, bilateral treaties), massive capital flight, and a repricing of sovereign risk. The programme does not address these second-round effects.

problems_ignored.problem: Institutional implementation mechanism within the existing constitutional framework
problems_ignored.significance: The programme explicitly rejects the possibility of measures being applied through presidential or legislative channels; implementation presupposes a revolutionary overthrow whose conditions and stages are not specified.

problems_worsened.problem: Compatibility with the European legal and financial framework
problems_worsened.mechanism: Expropriation without compensation of the banking and industrial sector violates the fundamental principles of the single market and the free movement of capital; would trigger immediate infringement proceedings and heavy financial sanctions that the programme does not address.
problems_worsened.reasoning: The models converge on the mechanism: rupture is asserted, so European legal incompatibility is intrinsic to the chosen trajectory, not a side effect.

execution_risks.risk: Capital flight and sovereign refinancing crisis
execution_risks.reasoning: The announcement of expropriation without compensation would trigger an immediate repricing of sovereign risk, capital flight not covered by the announced controls, and refinancing difficulties on existing commitments; no stabilisation tool envisaged.

execution_risks.risk: Absence of a constitutional implementation mechanism
execution_risks.reasoning: The programme explicitly refuses to be enacted through presidential channels; no institutional roadmap is provided, which makes any assessment of political feasibility impossible.
Expropriation without compensation, monopoly on foreign trade, and abolition of VAT contravene fundamental rules of the single market; financial sanctions and freezing of European funds are likely.

key_measures.measure: Automatic indexation of wages, pensions, and minimum benefits on the actual rise in prices (sliding scale); claimed minimum €2,000 net (2022)
Ban on layoffs in any profitable company or one paying dividends
Expropriation without buy-out or compensation of banks and large capitalist groups; single public banking service; public monopoly on foreign trade
Minimum 50% corporate tax for large groups; progressive abolition of VAT; end of unconditional public aid (CICE, misused CIR)

budgetary.note: Programme financeable only through expropriation and refusal of public debt; no aggregate costing or budget-balancing mechanism provided.

per_model.note: Lasting rise in spending without a costed budget framework.
Massive fiscal expansion (services, pensions, mass hiring) with no published budget assessment.
Revenue expected from expropriated capital; no published macro model.
Very heavy assumptions on capital revenue; no transition analysis.
No fiscal trajectory, no aggregate costing — sustainability undeterminable.
VAT abolition and mass hiring without budget-balancing arithmetic.

implementation.note: Expropriation without buy-out, forced indexation, and monopoly on foreign trade presuppose constitutional revision and legal mechanisms that do not exist.

per_model.note: No institutional roadmap within the Fifth Republic framework.
Implementation through revolutionary overthrow, outside the constitutional framework.
Simultaneous requisitions, expropriation, and planning exceed any existing administrative capacity.

dependency.note: Consistency of the scheme presupposes a collective uprising and the capacity to contain capital and markets; conditions outside the control of a government alone.

per_model.note: Depends on an institutional and international rupture.
Acknowledged dependence on a revolutionary overthrow; absence of institutional alternative.
Conditional on proletarian revolution; maximal dependence.
Depends on prior dismantling of the Fifth Republic and EU framework.
Full dependence on a non-programmable revolutionary uprising.
Relies on worker self-organisation as a precondition for implementation.

reversibility.note: Expropriation without buy-out and sovereign default would produce lasting effects on private capital and on confidence, hard to reverse in the short and medium term.

per_model.note: Very limited reversibility of expropriations without compensation.
Expropriation without buy-out and rupture with the EU difficult to reverse.
Revolutionary measures by construction not amenable to reform.
Dismantling of the Fifth Republic creates a lasting alternative trajectory.
Institutional overthrow by construction not amenable to reform.
Fiscal tools and SMIC amendable; expropriation hard to reverse.

headline.text: Immediate regularisation of ~700,000 undocumented workers, open borders, maximal social rights funded by expropriation and 100% Sécu.

per_model.text: Immediate regularisation, mass hiring in health/education, alignment of minimum benefits on the SMIC, full coverage by Sécurité sociale.
Unconditional regularisation, open borders, universal voting rights for residents, mass hiring in health/education; strong consistency but funding not specified.
Coherent social-progressive programme: regularisation, open borders, mass hiring, 100% Sécurité sociale, expanded women's rights.
Complete overhaul of the social model: open borders, unconditional regularisation, mass hiring in public services, women's rights, structural anti-racism.
Radical social-progressive demands (regularisation, open borders, women's rights) framed within class struggle rather than liberal identity politics.
Unconditional regularisation of ~700k, universal voting rights for residents, mass hiring in public services; funding through expropriation not costed.

social_demographic.summary: The social-demographic plank is one of the most developed in the programme. Five structuring levers: (i) immediate regularisation, with no condition or selection, of all undocumented workers (estimated at around 700,000, of whom 40–62% of the workforce in Île-de-France in home care, construction, hospitality, and cleaning), repeal of all immigration laws, maintenance and strengthening of birthright citizenship, voting rights for all foreign residents in all elections; (ii) reconstruction of the public health service through mass hiring in hospitals and care homes (EHPAD) at the level of needs identified by caregivers, reopening of closed beds/services/maternity wards, full coverage by Sécurité sociale funded by increased employer contributions, public coverage of dependency; (iii) overhaul of education through mass recruitment of teachers, prioritising working-class neighbourhoods, admission of children from age 2 in pre-school, refusal of social selection in higher education, abolition of Parcoursup; (iv) women's rights: real wage equality monitored by women employees, unconditional defence of abortion and contraception, massive resources against sexist violence, upgrading of "care" professions; (v) youth: full-time permanent contracts from the first job, student autonomy allowance, real free higher education, end of Parcoursup. Four models (chatgpt, deepseek, gemini, mistral) grade B noting internal consistency, the centrality of the social-security scheme, and the explicit articulation with the wage plank; claude and qwen grade C, stressing the absence of costing of the hires, of the cost of 100% Sécu, and of the conditions for application to foreign residents.

problems_addressed.problem: Administrative precarity and exploitation of undocumented workers
problems_addressed.approach: Immediate regularisation, with no condition or selection, of all undocumented workers (~700,000); open borders; repeal of all successive immigration laws; immediate halt to deportations; voting rights for all foreign residents.
problems_addressed.reasoning: Explicit, targeted, and quantified mechanism (estimate of 700,000); the six models converge on the identification of the flagship measure and on its consistency with the proletarian internationalist framework.

problems_addressed.problem: Collapse of the public health service and medical deserts
problems_addressed.approach: Mass hiring at public hospitals and care homes (EHPAD) at the level of needs identified by caregivers; reopening of closed beds, services, and maternity wards; removal of the doubling of medical co-payments; 100% Sécu coverage on all prescribed care; public coverage of dependency; local public health service against medical deserts.
problems_addressed.reasoning: The mechanism directly targets the identified cause (under-staffing, public disengagement); financing through increased employer contributions rather than direct tax hikes.

problems_addressed.problem: Reproduction of educational inequalities and social selection in higher education
problems_addressed.approach: Mass recruitment of teachers prioritising working-class neighbourhoods; admission of children from age 2 in pre-school with corresponding ATSEM hires; abolition of Parcoursup; refusal of social selection in higher education; real free higher education; autonomy allowance for students.
problems_addressed.reasoning: Combination of class-size reduction and financial autonomy schemes; equalising effect on human capital spread over the long term, not quantified.

problems_addressed.problem: Violence against women and wage inequality
problems_addressed.approach: Real and immediate wage equality monitored in each company by women employees themselves; fight against imposed part-time work; upgrading of care professions; unconditional defence of the right to abortion and contraception, free and local access; massive resources for associations, shelters, and the justice system.
problems_addressed.reasoning: Structural approach combining wage struggle, action against feminised precarity, and reproductive rights; costing of the resources is absent.

problems_ignored.problem: Long-term actuarial sustainability of the health and pension system in the face of ageing
problems_ignored.significance: The expansion of the Sécu perimeter (100% prescribed care, public dependency coverage) and retirement at 60 with 37.5 contribution years are stated without actuarial modelling; the demographic drift could outstrip the additional revenues expected from expropriated capital.

problems_ignored.problem: Reception capacity and migration flows under an open-borders regime
problems_ignored.significance: The programme states open borders as a principle without explicitly addressing reception capacity (housing, schools, health) or short-term effects on the labour market; no quantified estimate of expected flows.

problems_worsened.problem: Compatibility with EU law on voting rights and freedom of movement
problems_worsened.mechanism: Universal voting rights for foreign residents in all elections (including national ones) is legally incompatible with the current Constitution; implementation presupposes a constitutional revision not specified by the programme.
problems_worsened.reasoning: Four models flag the constitutional incompatibility; the programme does not propose the revision mechanism.

execution_risks.risk: Shortage of qualified personnel to absorb mass health/education hiring
execution_risks.reasoning: The needs identified by caregivers and teachers cannot be met without several years of prior training; the programme does not address the recruitment and training trajectory.

execution_risks.risk: Pressure on social housing and emergency shelter under an open-borders regime
execution_risks.reasoning: Open borders and unconditional regularisation would increase demand on an already-stretched social housing stock; the housing plank (requisitioning, rent controls, construction) may not absorb the demand in the short term.

key_measures.measure: Immediate regularisation, with no condition or selection, of all undocumented workers (~700,000); open borders; immediate halt to deportations; universal voting rights for residents
Mass hiring at hospitals, care homes (EHPAD), and in education at the level of needs identified by the personnel themselves
Full coverage (100%) by Sécurité sociale of prescribed care; financing through increased employer contributions; public coverage of dependency
Abolition of Parcoursup; real free higher education; student autonomy allowance indexed on the cost of living

budgetary.note: 100% Sécu, mass hiring, indexation, and universal voting rights weigh on the social accounts; financing referred back to systemic rupture.

per_model.note: Social perimeter significantly broadened with no modelled balance.
100% Sécu perimeter and mass hiring lastingly worsen the social-account balance.
Financing through employer contributions and expropriation of capital.
Lasting and structural increase in social spending.
Massive social expansion without an operational fiscal framework.
Autonomy allowance + free education + 100% Sécu without budget-balancing arithmetic.

implementation.note: Several measures require a constitutional revision (article 3) or heavy administrative arrangements; regularising 700,000 only feasible at large scale.

per_model.note: Recruitment/training capacity insufficient on a legislative timescale.
Mass regularisation simultaneous with public-services expansion.
Measures technically applicable under the proposed fiscal regime.
Simultaneous overhaul of health/education/migration without administrative precedent.
No precise enactment mechanism within the Fifth Republic framework.
Existing capacity can be expanded gradually with public recruitment.

dependency.note: Net effect depends on the administration's capacity to absorb regularisations, mass hiring, and 100% Sécu without saturating services.

per_model.note: European cooperation and constitutional revision required.
Constitutional dependence (voting rights) and sectoral cooperation (Sécu).
Depends on the revolutionary context but technically applicable.
Depends on prior dismantling of the Fifth Republic framework.
Full dependence on revolutionary institutional rupture.
Trade-union and hospital cooperation required for enforcement.

reversibility.note: Once acquired, expanded social rights are politically hard to repeal; regularisation produces status effects that are hard to reverse.

per_model.note: Hires and social rights amendable by a later legislature.
Regularisation produces administrative situations that are hard to reverse; public hires are lasting.
Social provisions amendable by a majority; regularisations hard to reverse.
Revolutionary framework gives rise to an alternative institutional trajectory.
Sécu framework and labour law amendable through ordinary legislation.

headline.text: Immediate NATO withdrawal, opposition to rearmament, internationalism without Frexit; no articulated alternative defence doctrine.

per_model.text: NATO withdrawal, opposition to the military budget, refusal of imperialist conflicts; rejection of national withdrawal and trade barriers.
Immediate NATO withdrawal, refusal of rearmament and trade war; symmetric rejection of EU federalism and national sovereigntism, with no alternative defence doctrine.
Anti-imperialist proletarian internationalism: NATO withdrawal, refusal to send troops, opposition to ongoing wars, with no Frexit or protectionism.
Maximalist rejection of the national framework: NATO withdrawal, open borders, refusal of trade barriers, proletarian internationalism as a guiding principle.
NATO withdrawal and rejection of the EU as a capitalist instrument with no defence counterpart; no operational security doctrine.
Anti-NATO, anti-imperialism, and rejection of rearmament; no strategic analysis of post-NATO defence autonomy.

security_sovereignty.summary: On security and sovereignty issues, the programme articulates three constant Lutte Ouvrière lines: (i) strict opposition to any increase in the military budget and to the diversion of social budgets toward armament (aircraft carriers, equipment), with immediate withdrawal from NATO, refusal to send troops or weapons into imperialist conflicts, a steady position against the war waged by Israel in Gaza (without alignment with Hamas), and frontal critique of US imperialism (Venezuela, Syria, Nigeria) and French imperialism (Africa); (ii) proletarian internationalism asserted as a framework — "workers have no homeland" — combined with symmetric rejection of the current EU (an instrument of European big capital) and of the national/sovereigntist withdrawal seen as a dead end for workers: no Frexit demanded, struggle for worker demands harmonised upward at the European level; (iii) refusal of trade war and customs barriers, seen as bearing on working-class people on both sides. Four models (chatgpt, claude, deepseek, gemini) grade C noting internal consistency and the historical stability of the line (constant since Laguiller); mistral grades F and qwen D, stressing the absence of any replacement defence doctrine after NATO withdrawal, the absence of a military budget plan, and the absence of articulation with existing international commitments (UN, European defence).

problems_addressed.problem: Diversion of social budgets toward rearmament and NATO
problems_addressed.approach: Opposition to any increase in the military budget; refusal of financing through cuts to health, school, and transport budgets; immediate withdrawal from NATO; refusal to send troops or weapons to imperialist conflicts.
problems_addressed.reasoning: The programme articulates an explicit budget trade-off between military and social spending; the six models converge on the identification of the lever.

problems_addressed.problem: Division of working-class people through securitarian migration policies
problems_addressed.approach: Permanent struggle against racism, Islamophobia, and antisemitism, framed as "poisons dividing the working class"; end of stop-and-search profiling and police surveillance of working-class neighbourhoods; repeal of successive immigration laws.
problems_addressed.reasoning: Class-based framing of internal security: lasting "security" is posited as a derivative of jobs and wages rather than of policing arrangements.

problems_ignored.problem: Alternative defence doctrine after NATO withdrawal
problems_ignored.significance: The programme proposes immediate withdrawal from NATO without articulating a replacement national or European defence doctrine; questions of deterrence, military cooperation, and security guarantees are not addressed.

problems_ignored.problem: Articulation with existing international commitments (UN, bilateral treaties)
problems_ignored.significance: The programme rejects NATO and criticises the EU but does not specify the position toward UN commitments, bilateral defence agreements (notably with Germany and the United Kingdom), or the doctrine for the use of nuclear forces.

problems_ignored.problem: Internal security and the fight against organised crime
problems_ignored.significance: LO notes that lasting security depends on jobs and wages but does not address organised crime, trafficking, and the security of working-class neighbourhoods as specific issues.

problems_worsened.mechanism: Unilateral NATO withdrawal combined with rejection of the EU and the announced economic rupture would place France in major diplomatic and strategic isolation; effects not addressed by the programme.
problems_worsened.reasoning: Four models flag the diplomatic isolation effect as intrinsic to the chosen trajectory.

execution_risks.risk: Vacuum of security guarantee after NATO withdrawal
execution_risks.reasoning: Unilateral withdrawal from NATO, with no articulated alternative doctrine, would create a vacuum of collective security guarantee for which the programme provides no operational answer.

execution_risks.risk: Diplomatic and economic sanctions in response to unilateral withdrawal and simultaneous expropriation
execution_risks.reasoning: The combination of NATO withdrawal + expropriation without compensation + open borders would expose France to coordinated counter-measures from Western partners.

key_measures.measure: Opposition to any increase in the military budget; refusal of financing through cuts to social budgets
Refusal of the current EU without Frexit; struggle for worker demands harmonised upward at the European level

budgetary.note: Opposition to rearmament reduces budgetary pressure on this line; expected savings on the military budget redirected to public services.

per_model.note: Expected budgetary savings on the military.
Reorientation of military budgets toward social spending eases budgetary pressure.
Refusal of rearmament caps military spending.
NATO withdrawal generates unquantified transition costs (defence autonomy).
Reduction of the defence budget claimed.

implementation.note: NATO withdrawal with no alternative doctrine; rejection of the current EU with no replacement framework; cooperation tools left in suspense.

per_model.note: NATO withdrawal with no alternative defence doctrine.
Articulation between NATO rejection, EU rejection, and internationalism not operationalised.
Procedural NATO withdrawal; the rest is declarative internationalism.
Simultaneous diplomatic rupture with NATO and the EU.
No operational roadmap for diplomatic transition.
NATO withdrawal procedure known; the rest of the doctrine is declarative.

dependency.note: The package (NATO withdrawal + EU rejection + internationalism) presupposes a transnational dynamic that is not within the power of a single state.

per_model.note: Depends on transnational dynamics outside any control.
Dependence on a European labour movement that has not materialised.
Unilateral NATO withdrawal is feasible; the rest depends on context.
Coherence of the package requires transnational mobilisations.
Full dependence on the international class struggle.
NATO withdrawal actionable on its own; the rest is subordinated to the revolutionary framework.

reversibility.note: NATO withdrawal procedurally reversible, but lasting diplomatic-reputation effects during the rupture period.

per_model.note: NATO withdrawal reversible at a political cost.
NATO withdrawal procedurally reversible; lasting reputation effects.
NATO withdrawal reversible by a later political decision.
Combination of NATO withdrawal + open borders creates a lasting alternative trajectory.
Simultaneous diplomatic rupture leaves lasting traces.

headline.text: Refusal of presidentialism, direct democracy in the workplace, recallable mandates; no post-rupture architecture.

per_model.text: Refusal of presidentialism and of the Vᵉ République; direct democracy in the workplace as an alternative; no replacement institutional model.
Symbolic testimonial presidency, openly assumed refusal to play the institutional game; no constitutional reform proposed.
Coherent opposition practice: refusal of presidentialism, recallable mandates, indemnity at the level of a skilled worker's wage, sovereign general assemblies in the workplace.
Direct, recallable workers' democracy: an articulated alternative to presidentialism, but with no formalised institutional framework.
Programme strictly declarative on the institutional plane: no replacement architecture for the Vᵉ République.
Refusal of presidentialism with a counter-proposal (direct democracy in the workplace) but no institutional transition plan.

institutional_democratic.summary: The institutional programme combines a refusal of presidentialism and of the Vᵉ République (Nathalie Arthaud's candidacy is explicitly "testimonial," a tool for propagating revolutionary communist ideas) with a counter-proposal centred on direct democracy in the workplace: sovereign general assemblies of workers, elected representatives paid at the level of a skilled worker's wage, imperative mandates revocable at any time, worker control over production and accounts. The programme strengthens public liberties (end of stop-and-search profiling, opposition to surveillance arrangements, defence of the right to demonstrate and to strike, freedom of expression and the press) and extends political rights to foreign residents (universal voting rights for all residents). DeepSeek and Gemini grade C, noting the internal consistency and the operational radicalism of direct democracy; the others grade D, pointing to the absence of any post-revolutionary institutional architecture (parliament, executive, judiciary) and of any roadmap for the transition between the current state and the claimed "democratic society of workers"; mistral grades F, stressing the strictly declarative character of the package. Universal voting rights for foreign residents directly conflict with article 3 of the Constitution (reservation of French citizenship for national elections), a conflict not addressed by the programme.

problems_addressed.problem: Concentration of presidential power and delegitimisation of countervailing powers
problems_addressed.approach: Openly assumed refusal of presidentialism; presidential candidacy used as a platform; counter-proposal of direct democracy in the workplace (sovereign general assemblies, recallable mandates, indemnity at a worker's wage level).
problems_addressed.reasoning: Five models recognise the consistency of the critique of presidentialism and of the operational counter-proposal at the company level.

problems_addressed.problem: Restriction of public liberties and surveillance of working-class people
problems_addressed.approach: End of stop-and-search profiling; repeal of mass-surveillance arrangements; defence of the right to demonstrate, the right to strike, freedom of expression and the press; opposition to demonstration bans.
problems_addressed.reasoning: Six models converge on the identification of a coherent framework for the defence of public liberties associated with popular mobilisations.

problems_addressed.approach: Universal voting rights for all residents (including non-EU foreigners) at all elections; regularisation of all undocumented migrants; repeal of immigration laws.
problems_addressed.reasoning: A strong measure for broadening the electorate, but in conflict with article 3 of the Constitution, an issue not addressed by the programme.

problems_ignored.problem: Post-revolutionary institutional architecture (parliament, executive, judiciary, constitutional review)
problems_ignored.significance: The programme calls for a "democratic society of workers" without specifying the organisation of public powers, the mode of designation of national representatives, the separation of powers, or constitutional review.

problems_ignored.problem: Mode of transition between the current state and the targeted society
problems_ignored.significance: The programme rests on a "class struggle" dynamic without specifying how to articulate that dynamic with existing institutions (elections, government, parliament) during the transition phase.

problems_ignored.problem: Guarantees for the rights of political minorities within the proposed framework
problems_ignored.significance: Direct democracy in the workplace, founded on sovereign general assemblies and recallable mandates, does not address guarantees for political or trade-union minorities, nor individual protections against majority decisions.

problems_worsened.problem: Constitutional legitimacy of the flagship measures
problems_worsened.mechanism: Several measures (expropriation without buy-out, voting rights for foreigners at all elections, imposed wage indexation) directly conflict with the current Constitution (articles 3, 17, 34) without the programme proposing any constitutional revision procedure or addressing the question of the institutional legality of the transition.
problems_worsened.reasoning: Four models identify the unaddressed risk of constitutional incompatibility as a factor of institutional fragility.

execution_risks.risk: Post-rupture institutional vacuum in the absence of a replacement architecture
execution_risks.reasoning: The call for a "democratic society of workers" with no institutional architecture (parliament, executive, judiciary) creates a risk of vacuum if implemented, with full dependence on the claimed self-organisation.

execution_risks.risk: Constitutional blockage of the flagship measures
execution_risks.reasoning: Expropriation, universal voting rights for foreign residents, forced indexation — all are arrangements that require at minimum a constitutional revision not envisaged by the programme.

key_measures.measure: Refusal of presidentialism; testimonial candidacy used as a platform
Direct democracy in the workplace: sovereign general assemblies, imperative and recallable mandates, elected representatives paid at a skilled worker's wage
Strengthening of public liberties: end of stop-and-search profiling, defence of the right to demonstrate and to strike, opposition to mass surveillance
Universal voting rights for all residents at all elections

budgetary.note: Institutional measures are essentially procedural and symbolic; no significant budgetary cost is identified.

per_model.note: Organisational measures with no significant cost.
Indirect costs of institutional reorganisation conceivable.
Procedural measures with no significant budgetary cost.

implementation.note: Several measures presuppose a constitutional revision not envisaged; post-rupture architecture not specified; direct democracy without legal framework.

per_model.note: Direct conflict with articles 3, 17, 34 of the Constitution.
No operationalisable legal framework for the transition.

dependency.note: Coherence presupposes popular mobilisation and self-organisation of workers outside state control, a dynamic that is not assured.

per_model.note: Depends on a massive popular mobilisation.
Self-organisation of workers not guaranteed by the state.
Programme actionable within the company independently.
Coherence of the package requires transnational mobilisations.
Everything depends on the claimed self-organisation.
Direct democracy in the workplace actionable independently.

reversibility.note: Constitutional revision theoretically reversible but politically costly; direct democracy established by law can be repealed by a majority.

per_model.note: Reversible by a later majority but lasting effects.
Measures undoable by a later majority.
Combination of expropriation + constitutional revision creates a lasting trajectory.
Lasting institutional effects once put in place.

headline.text: Critique of productivist capitalism, free transport, thermal renovation; no emissions target, refusal of carbon taxes.

per_model.text: Concrete measures (free transport, thermal renovation) coherent but with no costed climate-transition architecture.
Critique of capitalist productivism as the structural cause; rejection of market arrangements; no emissions target nor calendar.
Class-based approach: refusal of carbon taxes that weigh on households, transition through socialisation of the means of production.
Systemic articulation of the critique: productivist capitalism = environmental cause; concrete measures but no emissions plan.
No substantive treatment of the long-term environmental dimension in the analysed programme.
Ideological consistency with the anti-capitalist critique, but absence of an operational transition roadmap.

environmental_long_term.summary: The programme's long-term environmental approach rests on a structural thesis: the cause of ecological destruction is productivist capitalism, organised for private profit and incompatible with rational resource planning. The concrete levers are: (i) free urban public transport and massive development of rail (freight and passengers) at the expense of road and road haulage; (ii) a massive housing thermal-renovation plan funded by the state, integrated into the broad public-hiring programme; (iii) explicit refusal of carbon taxes, carbon markets, and pricing arrangements that LO considers shift the burden of the transition onto working-class people; (iv) opposition to large harmful projects (mega-basins, A69, motorways); (v) socialisation of the means of production set as a precondition for ecological planning. The programme contains no quantified emissions target, no carbon-neutrality calendar, no specific energy trajectory (electricity mix, position on nuclear), no costed financing plan for thermal renovation. ChatGPT and Gemini grade C, noting the internal consistency and the concreteness of the operational measures; three models (claude, deepseek, qwen) grade D, pointing to the absence of a costed transition architecture; mistral marks NOT_ADDRESSED, judging that the long-term dimension is not substantively treated. No model notes a quantified positive expected effect on emissions.

problems_addressed.problem: Carbon-emitting transport and access to mobility for working-class people
problems_addressed.approach: Free urban public transport; massive development of rail freight at the expense of road haulage; hiring in rail and maintenance.
problems_addressed.reasoning: Concrete, operationalisable measure articulated with a public-hiring plan; expected effect on the rail modal share and on access to mobility.

problems_addressed.approach: Massive housing thermal-renovation plan funded by the state; massive hiring in construction; public coverage for low-income households.
problems_addressed.reasoning: Lever identified as a priority with articulation to the public-hiring plan; absence, however, of any costing of the number of dwellings to be renovated.

problems_addressed.problem: Burden of the ecological transition weighing on working-class people
problems_addressed.approach: Explicit refusal of carbon taxes, carbon markets, and individual pricing arrangements; financing of the transition through expropriation and socialisation of the means of production.
problems_addressed.reasoning: Coherent class-based framing; relieves low-income households of the tariff burden, but with no costed alternative mechanism to orient behaviour.

problems_ignored.significance: The programme contains no quantified greenhouse-gas emissions target, no carbon-neutrality calendar, no articulation with European commitments (Fit for 55) or international ones (Paris Agreement).

problems_ignored.problem: Energy mix and position on nuclear power
problems_ignored.significance: The programme does not specify the strategy on the electricity mix, does not explicitly take a position on the extension of existing reactors, on EPR2, or on the development of renewables.

problems_ignored.problem: Adaptation and resilience to climate hazards
problems_ignored.significance: The programme does not address adaptation to ongoing climate change (heatwaves, droughts, floods, sea-level rise) nor the planning of resilience for infrastructure and territories.
The programme tackles the environment essentially through the climate-energy lens; biodiversity, agricultural soils, water resources, and chemical pollution are little or not addressed.

execution_risks.risk: Absence of a price signal to orient demand
execution_risks.reasoning: The explicit refusal of carbon taxes and carbon markets, combined with the absence of any quantified target, deprives the package of instruments to orient demand in diffuse sectors (road transport, heating, industry).

execution_risks.risk: Implementation of the concrete measures conditioned on systemic rupture
execution_risks.reasoning: The ecological transition is subordinated to the "socialisation of the means of production": the concrete levers (free transport, thermal renovation) remain applicable outside any rupture, but the claimed scale presupposes control of production.

key_measures.measure: Massive development of rail freight at the expense of road haulage
Massive housing thermal-renovation plan funded by the state
Explicit refusal of carbon taxes and carbon markets

budgetary.note: Massive thermal renovation and free transport generate a significant unquantified cost; financing referred back to systemic rupture.

per_model.note: Renovation plan and free transport significantly costly.
Cost absorbed by the expropriation of large property.
Costly measures with no specific financing plan.
Dimension little addressed — cost not assessable.

implementation.note: Absence of any quantified target, calendar, and energy trajectory; concrete operational measures, but at scale they presuppose the rupture.

per_model.note: Absence of energy trajectory and emissions targets.
Difficulty scaling up without targets or steering instruments.
Dimension little addressed — little to execute.

dependency.note: Transition at the claimed scale depends on the socialisation of the means of production; partial measures less dependent.

per_model.note: Depends on the claimed rupture to be carried through.
Subordinated to the socialisation of the means of production.
International and energy context not under control.
Strong dependence on the systemic transition.

reversibility.note: Infrastructure (renovation, rail) has a certain inertia; without a legal target, the trajectory remains undoable by a later majority.

per_model.note: Trajectory undoable by abandonment of the plans.
Plans abandonable by a later majority.
Trajectory undoable through revision of the plans.

magnitude_estimate.value: qualitative — not costed in the programme
magnitude_estimate.units: n/a (no aggregated quantification of the net intergenerational transfer)
magnitude_estimate.caveats: The programme combines massive pro-young transfers (housing, education, public services, thermal renovation) and massive pro-old transfers (retirement at 60 with 37.5 contribution years, 100% Sécurité sociale, indexation), all financed by expropriation without buy-out and refusal to repay public debt; the actuarial sustainability of pension commitments is not demonstrated and the absence of climate targets does not allow assessment of the bequeathed environmental debt.

fiscal.summary: Increase in net wages via SMIC at €2,200 net and indexation; income tax unchanged for low wages; progressive abolition of VAT, financeable through expropriation. A 25-year-old earning the SMIC would benefit directly.
fiscal.quantified: +~€700/month gross at the SMIC level as claimed (rise from ~€1,770 to €2,200 net)

housing.summary: Requisition of vacant dwellings, strict rent control and rent freeze, massive social-housing plan; expected effect strongly positive on access to housing for young workers and students.

pension_outlook.summary: Retirement at 60 with 37.5 contribution years guaranteed: for a 25-year-old who entered the labour market early, full-rate retirement at 60; for the others, an effective extension of the activity phase after the contribution years. Actuarial sustainability not demonstrated.

labor_market.summary: Massive hiring in public services (health, education, rail, thermal renovation); sharing of working time without wage cuts; ban on layoffs in profitable companies. Expected positive effect on youth employment, but conditioned on systemic rupture.

environmental_debt.summary: Refusal of carbon taxes and absence of emissions targets; massive thermal renovation and modal shift to rail; net effect on the bequeathed environmental debt uncertain in the absence of a costed trajectory.

impact_on_25yo_in_2027.narrative_summary: For a 25-year-old in 2027, the programme combines massive immediate gains (wages, housing, access to public services, prospect of early retirement) with strong dependence on systemic rupture for their financing. The bequeathed environmental debt remains undetermined for lack of emissions targets; the actuarial sustainability of pension commitments is not demonstrated.

fiscal.summary: Progressive abolition of VAT and indexation of pensions on prices: positive effect on the purchasing power of low-income retirees; no increase in levies on pensions.

pension.summary: Indexation of pensions on actual inflation; opposition to any cut or freeze; minimum old-age benefit aligned on the claimed SMIC (€2,200 net) — sharp increase for the lowest pensions.
pension.quantified: minimum old-age benefit at SMIC = €2,200 net

healthcare.summary: 100% reimbursement by Sécurité sociale, massive hospital hiring, free essential medicines: expected effect strongly positive on access to care for seniors.

impact_on_65yo_in_2027.narrative_summary: For a 65-year-old in 2027, the programme proposes a net material improvement: indexed and revalued pensions, expanded access to care, lighter indirect taxation. The sustainability of these commitments rests on expropriation and the refusal to repay public debt, the feasibility of which is not demonstrated.

horizon_matrix.dimension_note: Retirement at 60 / 37.5 contribution years + full indexation + minimum old-age benefit at the SMIC: massive pro-old transfers; actuarial sustainability not demonstrated.

h_2027_2030.note: Short term: immediate pension revaluation and return to 60 years for the longest contributors; strong positive effect for existing retirees.

per_model.note: Return to 60 + indexation: immediate positive effect.
Immediate revaluation; no short-term financing effect.
Flagship measure with a strong immediate effect on pensions.
Obvious positive effect in the short term.
Effect limited by the budgetary cost to come.
Short term positive, sustainability not guaranteed.

h_2031_2037.note: Medium term: tension between massive pro-old commitments and undemonstrated actuarial sustainability; depends on financing through expropriation.

per_model.note: Financing through expropriation supports the system.

h_2038_2047.note: Long term: very generous pension commitments not covered by an actuarial mechanism; risk of real erosion if the claimed rupture does not occur.

horizon_matrix.dimension_note: Refusal to repay debt + expropriation: massive transfer from creditors to working-class people; implicit debt (pensions, climate) not addressed.

h_2027_2030.note: Short term: cancellation of financial debt eases the interest burden but causes a market rupture; ambiguous outcome for younger generations.

per_model.note: Immediate easing of the interest burden.
Positive short-term effect dampened by market rupture.
Net positive effect in the short term.

h_2031_2037.note: Medium term: effects of sovereign default on public credit; loss of market access but absence of debt service.

h_2038_2047.note: Long term: implicit debt (pensions, climate), not costed, grows in the background; younger generations inherit the uncovered commitments.

per_model.note: Uncovered commitments weigh in the long term.

horizon_matrix.dimension_note: Concrete measures (free transport, rail freight, renovation) with a likely net positive effect; without emissions targets or a price signal in diffuse sectors.

h_2027_2030.note: Short term: positive effect of the concrete measures (free transport, renovation plan launched); scale conditioned on operational deployment.

h_2031_2037.note: Medium term: absence of targets and price signal limits the systemic reach; positive effect of the concrete infrastructure but persistent diffuse inertia.

per_model.note: Limits of the package come to the fore.

h_2038_2047.note: Long term: without targets or steering instruments, the emissions trajectory remains undetermined; the bequeathed environmental debt is not measurable.

per_model.note: Absence of a target compromises the long term.

horizon_matrix.dimension_note: 100% Sécu, massive hospital hiring, free essential medicines: strong positive effect expected for all age groups, particularly seniors.

h_2027_2030.note: Short term: massive hiring and 100% Sécu produce an immediate effect on access to care.

h_2031_2037.note: Medium term: positive effects maintained if financing is secured; actuarial tension with the growing cost.

h_2038_2047.note: Long term: robustness of the system conditioned on systemic rupture; otherwise possible degradation with demographic ageing.

horizon_matrix.dimension_note: Massive hiring in education, abolition of Parcoursup, free higher education, autonomy allowance for young people: massive pro-young transfers with an expected structuring effect.

h_2027_2030.note: Short term: massive hiring, abolition of Parcoursup, free education; strong immediate effect for young people.

h_2031_2037.note: Medium term: structuring effect maintained if staffing and financing are consolidated.

h_2038_2047.note: Long term: structuring effect on human capital conditioned on rupture; otherwise return to budgetary-efficiency logics.

horizon_matrix.dimension_note: Requisition of vacant dwellings, rent control and rent freeze, massive social-housing plan: massive pro-young transfers on access to housing.

h_2027_2030.note: Short term: rent control and freeze produce an immediate effect on solvency; requisitions increase available supply.

h_2031_2037.note: Medium term: risk of reduction in the private rental supply; positive effect maintained if the social-housing plan is consolidated.

h_2038_2047.note: Long term: positive effect if public investment in social housing offsets the exit of private investors; otherwise erosion of the housing stock.

per_model.note: Risk of erosion of the stock.

intergenerational.reasoning: The programme combines massive pro-young transfers (housing, education, public services, wages) and massive pro-old transfers (retirement at 60, indexation, 100% Sécu) financed by expropriation and the refusal to repay public debt. In the short term, the net effect for all age groups is positive on concrete material dimensions. In the medium-long term, sustainability depends entirely on the claimed systemic rupture: if it occurs, the system is consistent; if it does not occur, pension commitments and the implicit environmental debt create an uncovered burden passed on to subsequent generations. The absence of climate targets prevents assessment of the bequeathed environmental debt. The models converge on the qualitative direction (mixed) but diverge sharply on amplitude and long-term sustainability.

dissenting_views.view: Considers that the long-term dimension (climate, environment) is not substantively addressed and judges the net intergenerational balance more negatively than the other models, notably due to the absence of an actuarial mechanism on pensions and the financial rupture induced by sovereign default.

Considers that the claimed systemic rupture (expropriation, debt repudiation) makes the financing of both pro-young AND pro-old commitments coherent in the long term, and therefore grades the long horizon more positively than the majority.

counterfactual.status_quo_trajectory: Under the continuation scenario, France pursues the current trajectory: pension age pushed back, worsening social deficit, gradual deregulation of the labor market, accentuated financialization, growing precarity of unskilled employment, persistent pressure on the purchasing power of working-class households, widening intergenerational inequalities (housing, pensions, bequeathed environmental debt), progressive erosion of public services, European integration and NATO as structuring frameworks of foreign policy.
counterfactual.reasoning: Le programme propose une rupture systémique articulée (expropriation sans rachat, indexation automatique, retraite à 60 ans avec 37,5 annuités, sortie OTAN, refus du remboursement de la dette publique, refus du présidentialisme) qui modifierait la trajectoire sur les cinq dimensions analysées. La direction du changement est jugée « mixte » par cinq des six modèles : positive pour les classes populaires et les bénéficiaires immédiats des mesures sociales et redistributives, négative pour la stabilité institutionnelle, financière et diplomatique en l'absence de doctrine de transition. Le facteur déterminant est la dépendance entière du programme à un « soulèvement révolutionnaire » revendiqué : si la rupture systémique advient, le dispositif est cohérent en ses propres termes ; si elle n'advient pas, les mesures phares restent essentiellement déclaratives et la trajectoire effective serait peu modifiée par rapport au statu quo. Le caractère testimonial assumé de la candidature (« campagne de classe ») rend l'évaluation contrefactuelle structurellement difficile : le programme refuse la posture du gouvernement applicable depuis l'Élysée.

unsolved_problems.problem: Actuarial mechanism for financing full pensions at 60 / 37.5 contribution years
unsolved_problems.why_unsolved: The program guarantees retirement at 60 with 37.5 contribution years and full indexation without presenting an actuarial financing mechanism (contribution rate, target demographic ratio, scheme balance), deferring sustainability to expropriation and to refusal to repay public debt.

unsolved_problems.problem: Operational mechanics of expropriation without buyout and defense against market reactions
unsolved_problems.why_unsolved: The program calls for expropriation without buyout or compensation of large groups (banks, energy, multinationals) without specifying the legal procedure (constitutional revision, expropriation laws), protection against capital flight, or expected countermeasures from markets and partners.

unsolved_problems.problem: Defense doctrine and collective security architecture after NATO withdrawal
unsolved_problems.why_unsolved: Immediate withdrawal from NATO is not accompanied by an alternative defense doctrine (national autonomy, European defense, bilateral agreements), nor by articulation with nuclear deterrence or UN commitments.

unsolved_problems.problem: Post-rupture institutional architecture (parliament, executive, judiciary, constitutional review)
unsolved_problems.why_unsolved: The claimed « democratic society of workers » is not specified at the institutional level: no description of the organization of public powers, of the mode of designation of national representatives, or of the separation of powers.

unsolved_problems.problem: Emissions targets and long-term energy trajectory
unsolved_problems.why_unsolved: The program rejects carbon taxes and carbon markets without proposing quantified emissions targets, a carbon-neutrality calendar, or a strategy on the electricity mix (nuclear, renewables).

unsolved_problems.problem: Constitutional conflict of flagship measures not addressed
unsolved_problems.why_unsolved: Several measures (expropriation without compensation, voting rights for foreign residents in all elections, forced wage indexation) are in direct tension with the current Constitution (articles 3, 17, 34) without any revision procedure proposed.

unsolved_problems.problem: Transition trajectory between the current state and the claimed rupture
unsolved_problems.why_unsolved: The program rests on a collective uprising as a precondition for implementation, without specifying the steps or governmental levers mobilizable before this rupture.

downside_scenarios.scenario: Capital flight and financial rupture upon announcement of expropriation without buyout
downside_scenarios.trigger: Announcement or perception of imminent expropriation without buyout of large financial and energy groups.
downside_scenarios.reasoning: The absence of capital-protection mechanisms and of international coordination would make a massive flight and a rupture of financial markets near-certain, in turn triggering countermeasures on international payments.

downside_scenarios.scenario: EU sanctions and diplomatic isolation after unilateral NATO withdrawal + trade rupture
downside_scenarios.trigger: Conjunction of NATO withdrawal, refusal to repay public debt, and expropriation of large foreign-owned groups.
downside_scenarios.reasoning: The sequence of simultaneous ruptures with the main economic and strategic partners would expose the country to coordinated countermeasures (asset freezes, sanctions, suspension of agreements), with the program offering no response doctrine.

downside_scenarios.scenario: Constitutional blockage and supranational jurisdiction of flagship measures
downside_scenarios.trigger: Attempted implementation of expropriation, forced indexation, and universal voting rights without prior constitutional revision.
downside_scenarios.reasoning: The main measures require at minimum a constitutional revision; with no proposed procedure, their implementation would be blocked by the Conseil constitutionnel, the Conseil d'État, and European courts.

downside_scenarios.scenario: Pressure on private housing supply following the combination of requisition + rent freeze
downside_scenarios.trigger: Simultaneous implementation of the rent freeze, strict rent control, and requisition of vacant housing without an operational massive compensatory public plan.
downside_scenarios.reasoning: The exit of private investors without a ramp-up of public social housing would produce a contraction of supply, a shift onto the saturated social housing stock, and a perverse effect on access to housing in the medium term.

downside_scenarios.scenario: Failure of the « plan B » for lack of collective uprising and return to status-quo constraints
downside_scenarios.trigger: Absence of popular mobilization on the scale required by the program in the early years.
downside_scenarios.reasoning: Since the program posits the « uprising » as a precondition for realization, the absence of this dynamic would leave the administration facing unfinanceable commitments, de facto triggering a return to constrained managerial trade-offs.

downside_scenarios.scenario: Security-guarantee vacuum after NATO withdrawal without alternative doctrine
downside_scenarios.trigger: Effective NATO withdrawal without a negotiated alternative defense agreement.
downside_scenarios.reasoning: The absence of a replacement doctrine (autonomy, European defense, bilateral partnerships) would expose France to a collective-security-guarantee vacuum to which the program offers no operational response.

flagged_for_review.claim: Metadata anomaly in the raw deepseek file (provider/version) versus the model identification in the categorization
flagged_for_review.issue: The raw file `deepseek-manual.json` has model-metadata fields that do not match the file name or the canonical model list; reminder to address for transparency consistency.
flagged_for_review.suggested_action: Check the deepseek raw file metadata on the next run and correct either the raw file or the canonical model list.

flagged_for_review.claim: Ecological axis without plurality (modal_score null) due to a fragmented distribution -2/0/0/+1/+2/+2
flagged_for_review.issue: No majority plurality on the ecological axis: the models diverge strongly between a « refusal of punitive ecology as neutrality » reading and an « anti-capitalist planning as pro-environmental orientation » reading.
flagged_for_review.suggested_action: Human reviewer: choose between keeping modal=null (flagging the contested nature) or manually editing toward a median value (+1) with justification.

flagged_for_review.claim: Note environnementale long terme avec NOT_ADDRESSED partiel (mistral)
flagged_for_review.issue: Mistral grades NOT_ADDRESSED on the environmental_long_term dimension, in disagreement with the five other models that grade C to D. The mistral coverage note is marked « partial » as a result.
flagged_for_review.suggested_action: Human reviewer: confirm whether the program covers the long-term dimension sufficiently to justify a grade (consensus retained: D) or whether NOT_ADDRESSED better reflects reality (signal of a dimension little addressed).

flagged_for_review.claim: Constitutional conflict of flagship measures not addressed par le programme
flagged_for_review.issue: Several flagship measures (expropriation without compensation, universal voting rights for foreign residents, forced wage indexation) are in tension with the current Constitution (articles 3, 17, 34) without the program proposing a constitutional revision procedure.
flagged_for_review.suggested_action: Human reviewer: check whether the mention of this conflict should be strengthened in the global summary and in the institutional_democratic dimension.
